children.name: Motacilla
children.description: Wagtails — slender, long-tailed passerines named for their incessant tail-pumping, which serves as a signal of alertness to potential predators. The white wagtail (sädesärla) is one of Sweden's most familiar and beloved birds, nesting on buildings, farms, jetties, and bridges across the country. It is among the first migratory birds to return in spring — its appearance is greeted as a sure sign that winter is ending. Wagtails walk with an elegant, deliberate gait, chasing insects on short sprints along the ground. They are strongly associated with human habitation in Sweden, and the sight and sound of a sädesärla bobbing on a roof is embedded in Swedish cultural memory.

children.name: Motacilla alba
children.description: The sädesärla — Sweden's most beloved small bird and a symbol of spring's arrival. Nests on buildings, boats, and jetties throughout Sweden from Skåne to Lapland. The pied wagtail (M. a. yarrellii) is the British subspecies. Tail-pumping frequency increases when the bird is alert.

children.name: Motacilla flava
children.description: A migratory summer visitor to Swedish meadows, wetlands, and agricultural land. Distinctive yellow-green and olive plumage. Often found following livestock, catching insects disturbed by their hooves. Has more than 8 recognised subspecies with different head colour patterns.

children.name: Motacilla cinerea
children.description: The most elegantly coloured wagtail — grey above, brilliant yellow below. Always found beside fast-flowing water. A breeding bird in mountainous regions of Sweden. Its long tail gives it a particularly exaggerated pumping action. Often overwinters near open water in southern Sweden.

children.name: Motacilla aguimp
children.description: A boldly pied black-and-white wagtail of sub-Saharan Africa, closely associated with rivers, lakes, and human habitation near water. It forages along shorelines and damp ground for insects, often nesting in crevices in stone walls or under bridges. One of the most familiar garden birds across much of urban Africa.

children.name: Motacilla capensis
children.description: An unobtrusive grey-brown wagtail endemic to southern and eastern Africa, found along the edges of streams, dams, garden ponds, and even flooded lawns. It bobs its long tail rhythmically and runs after small invertebrates with quick bursts of speed. The Cape Wagtail is one of the most adaptable wagtails, thriving in suburban environments far from natural water.

children.name: Motacilla citreola
children.description: A strikingly lemon-yellow and grey wagtail that breeds in wet meadows, marshes, and tundra from Russia to central Asia, wintering across South Asia and East Africa. Males in breeding plumage have a brilliant yellow head and underparts contrasting with a grey back and black chest band. A rare but sought-after vagrant to western Europe.

children.name: Motacilla clara
children.description: A slender, long-tailed wagtail of fast-flowing rocky streams in the highlands of central and eastern Africa. Its pale grey plumage and white eyebrow give it a cleaner appearance than its close relatives. It perches on rocks in mid-stream and bobs its enormously long tail continuously, feeding on aquatic insects and small fish.

children.name: Motacilla flaviventris
children.description: The only wagtail endemic to Madagascar, found along rivers, streams, and coastlines across the island. It has striking black-and-white upperparts with a yellow-washed belly. Like all wagtails it is closely tied to water, catching insects along muddy margins with quick dashes and tail pumps. Its song is a simple series of high twitters.

children.name: Motacilla grandis
children.description: A large, boldly patterned black-and-white wagtail endemic to Japan and the Korean peninsula, where it inhabits rocky rivers and streams even in urban areas. It is the largest member of the pied wagtail group, with an especially long tail. Pairs maintain year-round territories along river courses and defend them vigorously.

children.name: Motacilla lugens
children.description: A northeast Asian pied wagtail, breeding along rivers in Kamchatka, the Kuril Islands, and Sakhalin, wintering along coastlines of Japan and Korea. It differs from the Japanese Wagtail in having a largely white face and less black on the breast. Its taxonomy has been debated and it is sometimes treated as a subspecies of White Wagtail.

children.name: Motacilla madaraspatensis
children.description: The largest pied wagtail, endemic to the Indian subcontinent, always found close to streams, rivers, ponds, and lakes. Its long, broad white supercilium distinguishes it from all other pied wagtails. It nests in crevices in river banks or walls and is a resident breeder throughout South Asia, absent only from the highest Himalayan elevations.

children.name: Motacilla samveasnae
children.description: A recently described (2001) wagtail of the middle and lower Mekong River in Cambodia, Laos, and Thailand, restricted to exposed sand- and rock-bars in large rivers. It has striking black-and-white plumage with a white forehead patch. Due to its very restricted range and dependence on undisturbed riverine habitat it is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Motacilla tschutschensis
children.description: The eastern counterpart of the Western Yellow Wagtail, breeding from northeast Asia to Alaska and wintering from Southeast Asia to Australia. It was split from Motacilla flava based on genetic and vocal differences. Several subspecies show a range of head patterns; the nominate tschutschensis breeds on the Chukchi peninsula.

children.name: Dendronanthus
children.description: Forest Wagtail — a monotypic genus comprising a single highly distinctive species that differs from all other wagtails in its arboreal habits, nesting in trees and foraging on forest floors. It wags its tail sideways rather than up-and-down, a unique behaviour among the family.

children.name: Dendronanthus indicus
children.description: The Forest Wagtail is unique among the family in foraging in shaded forest interior, walking on the ground and through leaf litter with a distinctive lateral tail-swaying motion rather than the typical up-and-down pump. It breeds in temperate and subtropical forests from India to China and Korea, wintering in Southeast Asia and the Andaman Islands. Its bold double breastband makes it unmistakeable.

children.name: Tmetothylacus
children.description: Golden Pipit — a monotypic genus containing one of the most spectacular members of the family, the brilliant golden yellow Golden Pipit of East African savannas. Unlike most pipits it shows sexual dichromatism, with males dazzlingly yellow and black and females more streaked olive-brown.

children.name: Tmetothylacus tenellus
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured passerines of the African savanna: breeding males are entirely canary-yellow below with a black breastband, and yellow-edged wing feathers flash conspicuously in undulating display flights. Found in dry thornbush and open acacia savanna from Ethiopia and Somalia south to Tanzania. It is nomadic, following rainfall and erupting in numbers in areas of good grass growth.

children.name: Macronyx
children.description: Longclaws — large, robust African pipits characterised by a broad yellow, orange, or pink throat patch bordered by a bold black necklace, and exceptionally long hind claws adapted for walking through tall grass. They occupy open grasslands, marshes, and mountain meadows across sub-Saharan Africa and superficially resemble North American meadowlarks through convergent evolution.

children.name: Macronyx ameliae
children.description: A handsome longclaw of moist grasslands and the margins of marshes in eastern and southern Africa, distinguished by its vivid rose-pink throat and broad black gorget. It walks through tall grass on its long legs and perches on grass stems to sing a far-carrying, melodious song. Named after Amelia Beaumont, wife of 19th-century naturalist George Beaumont.

children.name: Macronyx aurantiigula
children.description: A rare and localised longclaw with a vivid orange-yellow throat, confined to coastal grasslands and bushland in northeast Tanzania and southernmost Kenya around the Pangani and Tana river systems. It is considered Near Threatened due to its restricted range and ongoing habitat loss through agricultural expansion. Its song is a series of clear, ringing whistles.

children.name: Macronyx capensis
children.description: The most familiar longclaw of South Africa, a resident of moist grasslands, damp meadows, and grassy roadsides from the Western Cape north to Mozambique. Its brilliant orange-yellow throat and black necklace are unmistakeable; it forages on the ground for invertebrates and small reptiles. The Cape Longclaw is strictly a grassland bird and is sensitive to transformation of its habitat.

children.name: Macronyx croceus
children.description: The most widespread longclaw in Africa, found in moist grasslands, dambos, and marshy ground from Senegal east to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. Its brilliant yellow throat bordered by a broad black gorget flashes as the bird flushes, giving an impression resembling a tiny meadowlark. It perches on the tops of grass stems and fence posts to deliver its loud, clear whistling song.

children.name: Macronyx flavicollis
children.description: An Ethiopian endemic longclaw of the highland grasslands of the central Ethiopian plateau, where it is locally common in montane meadows and pastures above 2,000 m. Its yellow throat is less vivid than that of the Yellow-throated Longclaw, and the black chest gorget is thinner. Like all longclaws it is largely terrestrial, running through grass on its extraordinarily long hind claws.

children.name: Macronyx fuellebornii
children.description: A montane longclaw of the highlands of Malawi, Zambia, and Tanzania, inhabiting moist upland grasslands and grassy clearings above 1,500 m. It has a yellow throat patch paler than related species and brown-streaked upperparts that provide camouflage in dried grass. Named after Friedrich Fülleborn, a German physician and explorer who collected in East Africa in the 1890s.

children.name: Macronyx grimwoodi
children.description: One of the rarest and most poorly known longclaws, confined to the seasonally flooded grasslands of the upper Zambezi basin in western Zambia. Its vivid salmon-pink throat and black gorget are striking, but the species is rarely encountered due to its inaccessible habitat. Listed as Vulnerable, Grimwood's Longclaw is named after Ian Grimwood, British wildlife conservationist.

children.name: Macronyx sharpei
children.description: A Critically Endangered longclaw restricted to the highland grasslands above 2,300 m on the Kinangop Plateau and Mau escarpment in Kenya. It has a yellow throat and pink-washed flanks, and is among Africa's most threatened passerines due to conversion of its Afromontane grassland habitat to agriculture. Named after the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe.

children.name: Anthus
children.description: Pipits — streaky brown passerines of open habitats, the sparrows of the moor and meadow. Pipits can be difficult to identify by plumage but their songs and calls are distinctive — the meadow pipit gives a thin, insistent 'seep' in flight. The tree pipit (trädpiplärka) sings a wonderful descending song, delivered in a parachuting display flight from a tree perch — one of the joyful sounds of Swedish woodland clearings in spring. Meadow pipits are extraordinarily numerous across Scandinavian moorland and bogland and are the primary host of the common cuckoo in many areas. Pipits walk (rather than hop) and wag their tails gently, unlike the more vigorous pumping of true wagtails.

children.name: Anthus pratensis
children.description: Sweden's most numerous open-country passerine. Abundant on bogs, moors, meadows, and coastal grasslands. The primary cuckoo host in Scandinavia. In winter, large numbers move south; the most common migrant visible from Swedish coastal watchpoints in autumn.

children.name: Anthus trivialis
children.description: A summer visitor to Swedish woodland clearings, young forest, and heathland edges. Sings a rich, varied song from a treetop perch before parachuting down in a spiralling display flight with legs dangling and tail fanned. Arrives later in spring than meadow pipit.

children.name: Anthus cervinus
children.description: Breeds on Scandinavian and Siberian tundra — including northern Sweden's fells. The rusty-red throat is unmistakeable in breeding plumage. Migrates through coastal Sweden in autumn. A rare and sought-after sighting at Swedish seawatching points.

children.name: Anthus antarcticus
children.description: The only passerine endemic to Antarctica and its sub-Antarctic islands, confined to South Georgia and a few adjacent islands where it lives among tussock grass and coastal rocks. With the eradication of introduced rats from South Georgia, populations have recovered strongly. It forages on invertebrates in tussock and intertidal zones, and its chattering song fills the sub-Antarctic air in spring.

children.name: Anthus australis
children.description: A widespread, plain-plumaged pipit of open grasslands, paddocks, roadsides, and rocky areas throughout Australia, New Zealand, and many Pacific islands. It walks briskly on the ground pumping its tail and uttering sharp 'tseet' calls in flight. It is notable for thriving in heavily modified agricultural landscapes as well as natural grasslands.

children.name: Anthus berthelotii
children.description: A small, pale pipit endemic to the Macaronesian archipelagos of the Canary Islands and Madeira, found in a wide variety of habitats from sea level to high volcanic summits. It is one of the few true Macaronesian endemic passerines and is tamer than most pipits, often foraging around human habitation. Named after the French naturalist Sabin Berthelot who worked in the Canary Islands.

children.name: Anthus bogotensis
children.description: A compact, dark-streaked pipit of the high Andean páramo grasslands, found from Colombia and Venezuela south through Ecuador and Peru to Bolivia, typically at elevations above 2,500 m. It forages on the ground among bunch grasses and sedges and delivers its song from low perches or in short aerial displays. It is replaced by closely related species at lower elevations.

children.name: Anthus brachyurus
children.description: A diminutive, short-tailed pipit of moist highland and lowland grasslands in central and southern Africa, ranging from Angola and Zambia south to KwaZulu-Natal. Its very short tail distinguishes it from most other African pipits. It favours areas with short, dense grass and is typically secretive, flushing abruptly and dropping back quickly into cover.

children.name: Anthus caffer
children.description: A well-streaked pipit of open bushveld, savanna, and lightly wooded grassland in eastern and southern Africa. It is best identified by its habitat preference for areas with scattered trees and shrubs, its distinctive call — a buzzy 'tss-wheep' — and its habit of perching in bushes more readily than other pipit species in its range.

children.name: Anthus campestris
children.description: A large, sandy-brown, lightly streaked pipit of dry open habitats — steppe, sandy heathland, cultivated fields, and semi-desert. Breeds from western Europe and North Africa across central Asia, wintering in tropical Africa. It walks with a particularly upright, wagtail-like gait on its long legs, often on bare or sparsely vegetated ground.

children.name: Anthus chacoensis
children.description: A plain, streaked pipit of the Gran Chaco region of Argentina, Paraguay, and Bolivia, inhabiting open grasslands with scattered shrubs and dry savanna. It is closely related to the Hellmayr's Pipit but occupies lower, drier habitats. Its diagnostic call — a buzzy rising note — distinguishes it from sympatric species.

children.name: Anthus cinnamomeus
children.description: The most widespread and abundant pipit in Africa, found in open grasslands, cultivated areas, and airfields from the Sahel south to the Cape. Previously lumped with the Australasian Pipit as 'Richard's Pipit', it has a warm cinnamon-buff tone to the underparts in many populations. Its bold tail-pumping and habit of running before flushing are characteristic.

children.name: Anthus correndera
children.description: A widely distributed South American pipit of open pampas, grasslands, and Andean plateaus from Peru and Brazil south to Tierra del Fuego and the Falkland Islands. It is heavily streaked above and below, with a distinctive pale supercilium. The Falkland Islands population is notably bold and confiding compared to mainland birds.

children.name: Anthus crenatus
children.description: A large, dark, heavily streaked pipit of rocky grassland and montane terrain in Lesotho, the Drakensberg mountains of South Africa, and adjacent highlands. It is one of the largest African pipits and favours rocky outcrops and boulder-strewn hillsides. Its distinctive loud call and preference for high-altitude rocky terrain distinguish it from the similar African Pipit.

children.name: Anthus furcatus
children.description: A small pipit of open grasslands and campos cerrados of southeastern South America, from Brazil and Bolivia south to Argentina. Its short bill and rather plain face pattern distinguish it from the Yellowish Pipit. It typically inhabits areas with short, sparse grass cover and forages by walking and gleaning small invertebrates from the ground.

children.name: Anthus godlewskii
children.description: A large, pale pipit breeding on open steppe and semi-arid grasslands from Mongolia to north-central China, wintering in South Asia and East Africa. Its resemblance to Richard's Pipit makes identification challenging, but it can be separated by its shorter bill, different median-covert pattern, and call — a nasal 'pshee'. Named after Polish collector Ksawery Godlewski.

children.name: Anthus gustavi
children.description: A stocky, heavily streaked pipit that breeds on the boggy tundra and forest edges of northeastern Russia from the Pechora River east to Siberia, wintering in the Philippines and Borneo. Its two broad pale stripes on the mantle give it a distinctive 'braced' appearance. A rare but regular autumn vagrant to western Europe along the Arctic coast migration route.

children.name: Anthus gutturalis
children.description: A montane pipit confined to the alpine grasslands and subalpine meadows of New Guinea's central mountain ranges and the d'Entrecasteaux Islands. It is a robust, streaked pipit found at high elevations on grassy ridges and alpine tussock. Unlike most pipits it seems reluctant to flush, often crouching among the grass when approached.

children.name: Anthus hellmayri
children.description: A lightly streaked, warm-buff pipit of dry grasslands, open cerrado, and Andean valleys from Bolivia and Brazil south to Argentina and Chile. It is closely related to Correndera Pipit but is paler and less heavily streaked, favouring drier and higher-elevation grasslands. Named after the German-American ornithologist Carl Edward Hellmayr.

children.name: Anthus hodgsoni
children.description: A handsome, well-marked pipit breeding in boreal and temperate forests from Siberia to Japan, wintering in South and Southeast Asia. Its olive-green upperparts and boldly spotted underparts make it one of the more distinctive pipits. It feeds on the forest floor under trees and is a regular vagrant to western Europe in autumn. Named after Brian Houghton Hodgson.

children.name: Anthus hoeschi
children.description: A large, pale pipit of the Drakensberg and Maluti mountain ranges of southern Africa, found in montane grasslands and high plateaus above 1,800 m. It is a resident breeder in the highlands of Lesotho and adjacent South Africa, wintering at lower elevations in the eastern grasslands. Named after German ornithologist Herbert Hoesch.

children.name: Anthus leucophrys
children.description: A large, plain-backed pipit of open grasslands, lightly wooded savanna, and burnt areas across sub-Saharan Africa. Its unstreaked sandy-brown upperparts immediately separate it from most other African pipits. It walks with an upright posture, bobbing its head and pumping its tail, and often perches prominently on termite mounds or anthills to survey its territory.

children.name: Anthus lineiventris
children.description: A boldly streaked pipit of rocky hillsides, granite outcrops, and miombo woodland edges in eastern and southern Africa. Its heavily streaked underparts and preference for rocky terrain distinguish it from related species. It forages among rocks and low scrub, rarely venturing far into open grassland, and nests in crevices or under rock overhangs.

children.name: Anthus lutescens
children.description: A small, yellowish pipit of open grassy habitats, moist meadows, and campos in South America, from Panama south to Argentina. Its yellowish tinge to the underparts and fine streaking distinguish it from similar species. It is often found near water, foraging in short damp grass, and delivers its song in a short fluttering aerial display.

children.name: Anthus melindae
children.description: A poorly known, localised pipit restricted to coastal grasslands and grassy clearings near Malindi on the Kenyan coast and in adjacent coastal Tanzania. It is similar to the African Pipit but has a more distinctly marked face and different call. Due to its very limited range it is considered Near Threatened, with habitat loss from coastal development a key concern.

children.name: Anthus nattereri
children.description: A distinctive pipit with rich ochre-buff underparts and sparse streaking, confined to moist campo grasslands and gallery forest edges in south-central Brazil and adjacent Paraguay and Argentina. It is listed as Vulnerable due to severe ongoing loss of its native campo cerrado and Araucaria grassland habitats. Named after Austrian naturalist Johann Natterer.

children.name: Anthus nilghiriensis
children.description: A localised pipit endemic to the montane grasslands of the Nilgiri Hills and adjacent ranges in the Western Ghats of southern India, found above 1,500 m. It is a heavily streaked, dark pipit resembling the Tree Pipit but resident and sedentary in its shola grassland habitat. It is classified as Vulnerable due to grassland degradation from encroaching wattle plantations.

children.name: Anthus nitidulus
children.description: A lightly built, neatly streaked pipit of open grasslands, scrubby clearings, and roadsides across the Philippine Islands and Sulawesi. It is one of the few pipits in the Philippines and is the common open-country pipit of lowland areas. Its relatively fine bill and clean face pattern with a prominent pale supercilium distinguish it from vagrants.

children.name: Anthus novaeseelandiae
children.description: The common open-country pipit of New Zealand, found in farmland, coastal dunes, riverbeds, subalpine grasslands, and offshore islands. It is bolder and more confiding than most pipits, often approaching humans out of curiosity. Maori know it as 'pīpīwharauroa' (pipit) and consider it a messenger. Now split from the Australasian Pipit, it is endemic to New Zealand.

children.name: Anthus nyassae
children.description: A slender, lightly streaked pipit of open miombo and brachystegia woodland, found from Tanzania and Malawi south to Zimbabwe. Unlike most pipits it prefers the semi-shaded ground beneath tall woodland trees rather than open grassland. It forages in leaf litter and on bare ground between the trees, and its thin, high-pitched song is easily overlooked.

children.name: Anthus pallidiventris
children.description: A large, pale-bellied pipit of lowland rainforest clearings and forest edges in central Africa, from Cameroon and Gabon to the Democratic Republic of Congo. Its long legs and plain, pale underparts are distinctive among forest-zone pipits. It favours areas of recently cleared or burnt forest margins where grassy growth provides foraging habitat.

children.name: Anthus peruvianus
children.description: A small pipit of coastal lomas grasslands and open terrain in Peru and northern Chile, restricted primarily to the fog-watered desert grasslands that form along the Pacific coast. These ephemeral 'lomas' habitats bloom after coastal fog, supporting an unusual biota including this endemic pipit. It is closely related to the Yellowish Pipit.

children.name: Anthus petrosus
children.description: A dark, smoky-grey pipit of rocky coastlines, sea cliffs, and boulder beaches around the coasts of northwest Europe from Norway to Portugal. Its dark legs (shared with Water Pipit), smoky plumage, and entirely coastal habitat are diagnostic. The Scandinavian subspecies littoralis is lighter and migrates south; British birds are largely sedentary.

children.name: Anthus richardi
children.description: A large, long-legged pipit of open grasslands and steppe from central Siberia east to northeast China, wintering in South Asia and East Africa. Its considerable size, long hind claw, loud 'shreep' call, and upright wagtail-like stance are distinctive. Named in honour of Jules Richard, a French naturalist. It is the type species against which many similar Anthus pipits are compared.

children.name: Anthus roseatus
children.description: An elegant high-altitude pipit of alpine meadows and rocky tundra in the Himalayas and mountains of central Asia, breeding from Afghanistan to southwest China at elevations up to 5,000 m. In breeding plumage the pale rose-pink flush on the underparts is distinctive. It winters at lower elevations on grasslands and cultivation in the Himalayan foothills.

children.name: Anthus rubescens
children.description: The most widespread pipit of the New World, breeding in alpine tundra and Arctic latitudes from Alaska east to Newfoundland and wintering in agricultural fields and coastal grasslands across the southern United States, Mexico, and Central America. It walks in compact flocks, pumping its tail, and utters thin 'pip' or 'tsip' calls in undulating flight.

children.name: Anthus rufulus
children.description: The common, lightly streaked pipit of lowland South and Southeast Asia, found in paddyfields, grassy roadsides, open cultivated land, and coastal meadows from Pakistan east to the Philippines. It is one of the most abundant passerines in many Asian agricultural landscapes. Its diagnostic call — a sharp 'chit chit' — and habit of perching on fence posts and telephone wires are characteristic.

children.name: Anthus similis
children.description: A large, lightly streaked, pale pipit of rocky hillsides, scrubby grassland, and dry open terrain across Africa and South Asia, from Morocco and Ethiopia south to South Africa and east to India. Its notably long bill and relatively plain appearance distinguish it from many similar species. It perches prominently on rocks and termite mounds and has a loud, clear song.

children.name: Anthus sokokensis
children.description: A Critically Endangered pipit restricted to the Arabuko-Sokoke Forest on the Kenyan coast and a few adjacent forest patches in Tanzania, where it forages on the shaded forest floor under canopy trees. Its forest habitat preference is unique among East African pipits. Ongoing deforestation has reduced its population to fewer than 1,500 individuals, making it one of Africa's most threatened passerines.

children.name: Anthus spinoletta
children.description: A montane pipit that breeds in alpine meadows and rocky slopes above the treeline in the mountains of Europe and central Asia, descending in winter to coastal marshes, watercress beds, and muddy margins of lowland water bodies. In breeding plumage it is unstreaked below with a pinkish-buff breast, making it one of the more handsome pipits. Dark legs separate it from Meadow Pipit.

children.name: Anthus spragueii
children.description: A plain, buffy-white pipit of native short-grass prairie in the Great Plains of North America, from Alberta and Saskatchewan south to Texas on its breeding grounds, wintering in the southern USA and Mexico. The male performs a spectacular territorial song flight, circling high above the prairie for minutes while delivering its descending tinkling song. Named after Isaac Sprague, an American artist-naturalist.

children.name: Anthus vaalensis
children.description: A large, plain, warm-buff pipit of open dry grasslands, Kalahari shrubveld, and sparse Acacia savanna across southern and east-central Africa. Its uniform buffy colouration with little streaking above and below distinguishes it from most African pipits. It walks with a very upright posture and frequently bobs its tail. Often encountered on dusty tracks and airstrips in the semi-arid interior.
